title: Temperature Effects on Fish Schooling
text:
Project Overview
Temperature is highly influential on the physiology and behavior of ectotherms, but its specific impact on complex social interactions—such as schooling behavior as a defense against predation—is difficult to quantify.
This research investigated how temperature affects the ability of schooling fish to collectively respond to a predator. We subjected schools of fish to simulated looming predation threats under varying temperature conditions to track their maximum speed, acceleration, and startle latency.


Key Findings

Context-Dependent Optimal Temperatures: While speed and acceleration consistently showed a positive curvilinear response to temperature, the optimal temperature at which performance peaked shifted dramatically depending on whether the fish were unperturbed or actively avoiding a threat.
Behavioral Compensation: Our results suggest that ectothermic fish can compensate for slower swim speeds at lower temperatures by artificially increasing their sensitivity to startle stimuli during predation threats.

Read the full paper: Oikos (2023)

title: Forestry effects on Pacific salmon
text:
Changes in the structure and functioning of watersheds from forestry can affect the survival and growth of salmon during their freshwater life stages through various mechanisms.
We used a multi-stock hierarchical Bayesian model to assess the impact of historical clearcut logging in watersheds on salmon productivity across coastal BC as part of a collaborative project with Musgamagw Dzawada’enuxw Fisheries Group, Salmon Coast Society, University of Toronto, Trinity Consultants, Pacific Salmon Foundation, and Department of Fisheries and Ocean.

title: Parameter Estimation in Fishery Models
text:
We used simulations, profile likelihood, and Bayesian hierarchical modelling to check the limitations of commonly used fisheries models, such as the Ricker and the Beverton-Holt model.
Stay tuned for more.

title: Neuromodulation of Drosophila Flight
text:
Project Overview
Insect flight is a complex behavior requiring the real-time integration of multiple sensory inputs with flight motor output. While previous genetic studies identified central brain monoaminergic neurons that modulate flight in Drosophila melanogaster, the specific neuro-modulatory circuits underlying sustained, extended flight bouts remained unexplored.
This study focused on how monoaminergic modulation of GABAergic output neurons in the mushroom body (a higher integrating center in the insect brain) regulates the duration of flight bouts.


Key Findings

Circuit Identification: We discovered that octopaminergic neurons stimulate central dopaminergic neurons, which in turn project to GABAergic mushroom body output neurons (MBONs).
The ‘Flight State’: Inhibiting these octopaminergic/dopaminergic neurons, or activating the GABAergic MBONs, actively reduces flight duration. We propose that the disinhibition of this neural circuit acts as a “released brake” after flight initiation, allowing the fly to maintain an extended flight state to search for distant food sources or mates.

Read the full paper: Current Biology (2019)

title: Observing feeding behavior of Belted Kingfishers
text:
Project Overview
Belted Kingfishers (Megaceryle alcyon) are widely distributed across aquatic environments in North America, yet their behavior in marine habitats has remained understudied, particularly during the critical post-fledging period.
For this study, we recorded the daily activity budgets of adult and fledgling Belted Kingfishers across marine environments on San Juan Island, Washington. We specifically investigated how environmental factors (like tides) and age class impacted their marine feeding behaviors and success rates.


Key Findings

Tidal Influence on Hunting: Kingfishers exhibited the highest feeding attempt rate at high tide, but their actual strike success rate peaked at low tide.
Age and Experience: Adults foraged significantly more successfully than juveniles and captured fish that were nearly twice as large, highlighting the effects of juvenile inexperience during the post-fledging learning phase.

Read the full paper: The Wilson Journal of Ornithology (2021)

title: Salmon migration
text:
Project Overview
The large-scale seasonal migrations undertaken by many species require complex navigational and timing decisions. While the environmental factors influencing migration timing are well studied, the role of social interactions remains poorly understood.
In this project, we compiled 19 years of trap data from three rivers in Washington state to analyze the downstream seaward migration of juvenile Chinook and coho salmon. Using state-space statistical models, we tested the “pied-piper” hypothesis: that large numbers of migrating hatchery-origin salmon act as a social cue to stimulate the migration of wild salmon.


Key Findings

Collective Decision Making: An increase in hatchery salmon counts was a strong predictor for increased migration of wild salmon, acting alongside abiotic factors like temperature, photoperiod, and river flow.
Conservation Implications: Incorporating social information allows animals to benefit from collective decision-making in noisy environments. Understanding how large-scale hatchery releases impact wild populations provides crucial insights for planning the timing and duration of future management efforts.

Read the full paper: Movement Ecology (2026)
